title: Custom volumes configuration in containers.conf is ignored if a container is started with additional volumes
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

The customized [containers]::volumes in /etc/containers/containers.conf file setting is disregarded if a container is started with additional container specific volumes. The setting is honored only when no volumes are specified at the time of starting containers.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Customize /etc/containers/containers.conf
[containers]
volumes = ["/dev/log:/dev/log",]

2. Start a container with some volumes using `podman run -v ...` option

**Describe the results you received:**

`podman inspect` does not show the /dev/log bind mounted

**Describe the results you expected:**

`podman inspect` should bind mount /dev/log from host inside container

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.2
API Version:  4.0.2
Go Version:   go1.17.7

Built:      Tue Mar 15 15:15:06 2022
OS/Arch:    linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
Hi @vikas-goel , Thanks for creating the issue.

I think containers.conf is usually configured to set default values and any cli flags we set usually tends to override it but If we want to keep default volumes if they are configured then above PR should close this when it is approved and merged by maintainers https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/14027

> I think containers.conf is usually configured to set default values and any cli flags we set usually tends to override

After checking this issue again above issue requests for a feature that does not aligns with how defaults are expected to work with `containers.conf` afaik, if a default is configured and cli overrides it using `-v` should newly added volume should override the default or should it get merged with default ?  @containers/podman-maintainers WDYT

Also https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/docs/containers.conf.5.md explicitly states that it is a default configuration file so if we do anything like `-v` I think we are requesting not to follow the default hence I don't think we could do this as of now since that would leave no way for users to override `containers.conf` they will always inherit the volumes which are meant to be the defaults in `containers.conf`.

But lets wait for other maintainers to reply on this issue.
I guess for default mounts there is already the `mounts.conf` file

@Luap99 Yes thanks `mounts.conf` solves what is requested in this issue verified with upstream `podman`. 

@vikas-goel I think you should use `mounts.conf` for this exact problem I don't think definition of `containers.conf` aligns with the use-case but please feel free re-open this issue if you still think this needs to be fixed for `containers.conf`.

@vikas-goel: Reopened this issue.

<details>

In response to [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14025#issuecomment-1111405561):

>/reopen


Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

I am observing inconsistent behavior of podman with respect to custom settings in containers.conf. For example, my  /etc/containers/containers.conf file has the below entries:
```
[containers]

default_capabilities = [
  "AUDIT_WRITE",
  "MKNOD",
  "NET_RAW",
  "CHOWN",
  "DAC_OVERRIDE",
  "FOWNER",
  "FSETID",
  "KILL",
  "NET_BIND_SERVICE",
  "SETFCAP",
  "SETGID",
  "SETPCAP",
  "SETUID",
  "SYS_CHROOT"
]

default_sysctls = [
  "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding=0",
  "net.ipv4.ip_forward=0",
  "net.ipv4.ping_group_range=0 0",
  "net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=0",
]
```

When I start a container with `podman run --cap-add=SYS_ADMIN --sysctl "net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time=900"`, then both settings (in containers.conf and podman run) are honored.

Is the `volumes` only exception to that?

This seems to be working :)
I was comparing the inode of /dev/log inside container and on host. Whereas, it is /dev/log inside container and /run/systemd/journal/dev-log on host.
